---
title: "Methods Proposal and Outline"
author: "Yiming Cao, Zichen Fan, Xinyin Miao, Teresa Sha"
date: "2025-03-25"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
---

## EDA recap and motivation

From the EDA we found strong seasonal and day-of-week effects on mental health EMS calls, a nonlinear temperature–outcome relationship, and only a weak PM2.5 signal in raw scatter plots. The data has 124 observations (EPA monitors sample every 3 days) and the outcome is a daily count (mental_health mean ~486, suicide mean ~5), so we need a count regression that can handle overdispersion without overfitting on a small n.

This motivates a quasi-Poisson time series GLM — it handles overdispersed counts, lets us flexibly adjust for time trends and temperature with splines, and only requires base R packages.

We plan to do a nonlinear exposure–response analysis within the quasi-Poisson time-series framework. By replacing the linear PM2.5 term with a spline function, we can evaluate whether the association is approximately linear, whether there is evidence of a threshold, and whether the slope becomes steeper at higher PM2.5 concentrations.

## Quasi-Poisson GLM — analysis plan (Yiming Cao)

### Model

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{PM2.5}_t + \text{ns}(\text{time}, df) + \text{ns}(\text{tmean}_t, 3) + \beta_2 \cdot \text{is\_weekend}_t + \beta_3 \cdot \text{precip}_t
$$

where $Y_t \sim \text{Quasi-Poisson}$. Same structure for both outcomes (mental_health and suicide).

### Covariate selection

- **ns(time, 4)**: natural spline to capture the seasonal trend seen in EDA. ~1 df per 3 months.
- **ns(tmean, 3)**: nonlinear temperature adjustment. EDA showed a curved relationship that a linear term wouldn't capture, 3 df should be sufficient given n = 124.
- **is_weekend**: binary indicator. EDA showed a clear weekday/weekend split (~510 vs ~425). Using full factor(weekday) would cost 6 df which seems excessive for 124 obs.
- **precip**: linear term as a nuisance covariate — precipitation may affect both air quality and behavior.
- Not including wind speed or RH to avoid collinearity issues with the small sample size.

### R packages

`stats` (glm), `splines` (ns), `ggplot2` (figures).

### Sensitivity analyses

- Vary time trend spline df from 3 to 6 to check whether the PM2.5 estimate is sensitive to how much seasonal variation we absorb
- Exclude high-PM2.5 days (>95th percentile) that are likely wildfire smoke events, which represent a different exposure pattern
- Test lagged temperature (previous measurement day) instead of same-day

### Expected visualizations

- Rate ratio forest plot: PM2.5 effect (per 10 μg/m³) with 95% CI for both outcomes
- Sensitivity plot showing how the PM2.5 coefficient changes across different time trend df choices
- Residual diagnostics: deviance residuals vs fitted, PACF of residuals to check for remaining autocorrelation

## Methods section outline

- Quasi-Poisson GLM to estimate association between same-day PM2.5 and mental health EMS call counts in NYC, 2024
- Primary outcome: daily mental health crisis calls (EDP + ALTMEN); secondary: suicide-related calls (JUMPDN + JUMPUP)
- Exposure: city-wide mean PM2.5 from EPA FRM monitors
- Adjusted for: seasonal/long-term trend (natural spline, 4 df), temperature (natural spline, 3 df), weekend indicator, precipitation
- Results as rate ratio per 10 μg/m³ PM2.5 increase with 95% CIs
- Sensitivity: time trend df (3–6), excluded wildfire days, lagged temperature

## Team assignments

| Method | Person | Status |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Quasi-Poisson GLM | Yiming Cao | in progress |
| Mixed Model | Zichen Fan | — |
| Case-crossover | Teresa Sha | — |
| Nonlinear analysis | Xinyin Miao | in progress |



## Nonlinear exposure–response analysis — analysis plan (Xinyin Miao)


### Model

Primary nonlinear model:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \beta_0 + \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t}, 3) + \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{time}_t, 4) + \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{tmean}_t, 3) 
+ \beta_1 \cdot \mathrm{is\_weekend}_t + \beta_2 \cdot \mathrm{precip}_t
$$

where
$$
Y_t \sim \text{Quasi-Poisson}, \qquad \mu_t = E(Y_t).
$$

For comparison, the linear specification from the primary model will also be fit:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t}
+ \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{time}_t, 4)
+ \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{tmean}_t, 3)
+ \beta_2 \cdot \mathrm{is\_weekend}_t
+ \beta_3 \cdot \mathrm{precip}_t
$$


### Covariate selection

- **ns(PM2.5, 3)**: natural spline for PM2.5 to allow a flexible exposure–response curve.
- **ns(time, 4)**: natural spline to control for seasonal and long-term temporal patterns observed in the EDA.
- **ns(tmean, 3)**: nonlinear temperature adjustment, since EDA suggested a curved temperature–outcome relationship.
- **is_weekend**: binary weekend indicator to account for the clear weekday/weekend pattern in call volume while conserving degrees of freedom.
- **precip**: linear adjustment for precipitation as a nuisance weather covariate that may affect both ambient conditions and human activity patterns.

### R packages

`stats` (glm), `splines` (ns), `ggplot2` (figures).

### Analysis goals

- Evaluate whether the PM2.5–outcome association departs from linearity.
- Visualize the exposure–response curve for PM2.5 after adjustment for temporal trend, temperature, weekend, and precipitation.
- Assess whether there is evidence of a threshold effect; a steeper slope at higher PM2.5 concentrations; little change across the lower exposure range.
- Compare the nonlinear PM2.5 model with the linear PM2.5 model to determine whether allowing nonlinearity meaningfully changes interpretation.

### Sensitivity analyses

- Vary PM2.5 spline complexity from df = 3 to df = 4 to assess whether the estimated curve shape is robust
- Exclude high-PM2.5 days (>95th percentile) that may reflect wildfire smoke episodes and evaluate whether the nonlinear shape is driven by a few extreme observations
- Repeat the nonlinear model for both outcomes and compare whether the curve is more stable for mental_health than for suicide-related calls
- As an additional check, refit the nonlinear PM2.5 model under alternative time-trend df choices (3–6) to confirm that the exposure–response shape is not overly sensitive to temporal adjustment

### Expected visualizations

- Exposure–response curve for PM2.5 with 95% confidence intervals, shown on the rate ratio scale relative to a reference PM2.5 value
- Overlay plot comparing the fitted linear and nonlinear PM2.5 relationships
- Sensitivity plot showing how the PM2.5 curve changes under alternative spline df choices or after excluding extreme high-exposure days
- Distribution plot of PM2.5 values to show where the data are dense versus sparse, helping interpret uncertainty in the tails of the curve

### Methods section outline

- Nonlinear exposure–response analysis conducted within a quasi-Poisson time-series framework to evaluate whether the association between same-day PM2.5 and EMS call counts is nonlinear
- Primary outcome: daily mental health crisis calls (EDP + ALTMEN); secondary outcome: suicide-related calls (JUMPDN + JUMPUP)
- Exposure modeled using a natural spline for city-wide mean PM2.5
- Adjusted for: seasonal/long-term trend (natural spline for time, 4 df), temperature (natural spline, 3 df), weekend indicator, and precipitation
- Linear and nonlinear PM2.5 specifications compared to assess departure from linearity
- Results presented as adjusted exposure–response curves and rate ratios relative to a reference PM2.5 concentration
- Sensitivity analyses: PM2.5 spline df (3–4), alternative time-trend df (3–6), exclusion of extreme high-PM2.5 days






## Case-crossover sensitivity analysis — analysis plan (Teresa Sha)

### Model

As a supplementary sensitivity analysis, we will apply a **time-stratified case-crossover design** to evaluate whether the estimated short-term association between PM2.5 and EMS outcomes is robust to an alternative acute-effect framework.

Because the data are aggregated at the daily level, this design will be implemented using a **conditional Poisson regression model**, which is equivalent to a time-stratified case-crossover analysis for count data. Control days will be selected within the same **year, month, and day of week** as each case day, so that long-term trends, seasonality, and weekday effects are controlled by design.

For the primary case-crossover sensitivity model, we will fit:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \alpha_{s(t)} + \beta_1 \mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t} + \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{tmean}_t, 3) + \beta_2 \mathrm{precip}_t
$$

where

$$
Y_t \sim \text{Quasi-Poisson}, \qquad \mu_t = E(Y_t)
$$

and $\alpha_{s(t)}$ denotes the stratum-specific effect for the matched time stratum defined by year, month, and day of week.

To assess potential delayed effects, a lagged exposure model will also be considered:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \alpha_{s(t)} + \beta_1 \mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t} + \beta_2 \mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t-1}
+ \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{tmean}_t, 3) + \beta_3 \mathrm{precip}_t
$$

To evaluate possible effect modification by heat, we will additionally fit:

$$
\log(\mu_t) = \alpha_{s(t)} + \beta_1 \mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t}
+ \beta_2 \mathrm{heat\_day}_t
+ \beta_3 \bigl(\mathrm{PM}_{2.5,t} \times \mathrm{heat\_day}_t\bigr)
+ \operatorname{ns}(\mathrm{tmean}_t, 3) + \beta_4 \mathrm{precip}_t
$$

This analysis will not serve as the primary design. Instead, it will be used as a **robustness check** alongside the main time-series analysis.

### Covariate selection

- **PM2.5**: main short-term exposure of interest.
- **Stratum (year × month × day of week)**: matching factor in the time-stratified case-crossover design, used to control for seasonal pattern, long-term temporal trend, and weekday structure by design.
- **ns(tmean, 3)**: natural spline for mean temperature to allow a flexible adjustment for nonlinear temperature effects.
- **precip**: linear adjustment for precipitation as a nuisance weather covariate that may influence both ambient conditions and EMS activity.
- **PM2.5 lag 1**: included in a lagged model to assess whether the association extends beyond the same day.
- **heat_day**: binary indicator used to test whether the short-term PM2.5 effect is stronger on unusually hot days.

### R packages

`gnm` (conditional Poisson regression), `stats` (model fitting), `splines` (natural splines), `dplyr` (data management).

### Analysis goals

- Evaluate whether the short-term association between PM2.5 and EMS outcomes remains similar under a case-crossover framework.
- Use an alternative acute-effect design to reduce concern that the main findings are driven by modeling choices specific to the time-series approach.
- Examine whether there is evidence of a same-day PM2.5 effect, a lagged effect, or stronger associations during hot days.
- Compare the overall interpretation from the case-crossover sensitivity analysis with the primary time-series results.

### Sensitivity analyses

- Fit the same-day PM2.5 model first, then refit with **lag 1 PM2.5** to evaluate delayed short-term effects.
- Repeat the analysis for both outcomes: **mental_health** and **suicide-related calls**.
- Compare models with and without precipitation adjustment to check whether weather covariate choice meaningfully changes the PM2.5 estimate.
- As an additional check, compare the magnitude and direction of the PM2.5 association from the case-crossover approach with the main time-series model.

### Expected visualizations

- Table of estimated PM2.5 coefficients or rate ratios from the case-crossover models.
- Side-by-side comparison table of PM2.5 effect estimates from the **time-series** and **case-crossover** analyses.
- Simple coefficient plot showing point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for:
  - same-day PM2.5
  - lag 1 PM2.5
  - PM2.5 effect under hot-day interaction models

### Methods section outline

- A time-stratified case-crossover design will be used as a **sensitivity analysis** for short-term PM2.5 effects on EMS outcomes.
- Because the data are aggregated to the daily level, the case-crossover analysis will be implemented using **conditional Poisson regression** rather than individual-level conditional logistic regression.
- Case and control days will be matched within the same **year, month, and day of week**.
- The primary exposure is daily PM2.5 concentration; temperature and precipitation will be adjusted for as time-varying covariates.
- Additional models will assess **lag 1 PM2.5** and **interaction with heat-day status**.
- Findings from this sensitivity analysis will be compared with the primary time-series analysis to assess robustness.
